meta.title: CVE-2020–1088 — Yet another arbitrary delete EoP

tech_stack_fingerprints: Registry keys under HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Error Reporting\EtwSessions and DynamicEtwSessions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an ETW session trace is initiated and a specific file operation (rename then delete on failure) within a user-controlled temporary directory is performed by the SYSTEM-privileged Windows Error Reporting Service.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to trigger an application crash to initiate the WER process.
Attacker must have write access to the temporary directory where the ETW session files are created.
applicability_signals: Existence of ETW session configuration in the registry (`HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Error Reporting\EtwSessions` or `DynamicEtwSessions`).
WerSvc service running with SYSTEM privileges.
Creation of temporary files with `.buf.etl` extension in user-writable temporary directories (`%UserProfile%\AppData\Local\Temp\{GUID}`).
skip_if: The target system has been patched against CVE-2020-1088.
The Windows Error Reporting service is not running or is disabled.
ETW sessions are not configured or are not being initiated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if ETW sessions are configured in the registry by inspecting `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Error Reporting\EtwSessions` and `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Error Reporting\DynamicEtwSessions`.
Step 2: Verify the execution of `WerSvc` with SYSTEM privileges using tools like Process Explorer or Tasklist.
Step 3: Monitor the user's temporary directory (`%UserProfile%\AppData\Local\Temp\`) for the creation of GUID-named folders and files with `.buf.etl` extensions when an application crash occurs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify or create a target file that the attacker wishes to delete. This file should be located in a path that the attacker can control and where `WerSvc` might eventually perform operations.
Step 2: Trigger an application crash (e.g., by running a deliberately faulty executable or a known crashing application) to initiate the WER process and create the temporary ETW session file.

Step 3: While the ETW trace file (`.buf.etl`) is being processed and before it's renamed to `.etl` or deleted, create a symbolic link at the location of the temporary file pointing to the target file the attacker wants to delete.

Step 4: The `CWerService::SvcCollectETWLogs` function will attempt to rename the file. If this rename operation fails (which it likely will due to permissions or the presence of the symbolic link), the `CEtwSession::FlushBuffered` function will attempt to delete the original `.buf.etl` file. Because of the symbolic link, this delete operation will target the attacker's chosen file.
tools_recommended: Process Explorer or Tasklist: To verify service privileges.
Sysinternals Suite (e.g., Procmon): For detailed monitoring of file operations and symbolic link creation.
Custom C++ or PowerShell script: To reliably trigger application crashes and create symbolic links in a race condition.
success_indicators: The target file specified by the attacker is deleted.
No error messages related to WER are visible to the user, but the target file is gone.
failure_indicators: The target file is not deleted. This could be due to incorrect timing of symbolic link creation, the ETW session not being configured, or the WER process failing to create the temporary file. Pivot to checking other WER vulnerabilities or focusing on alternative arbitrary file operation vulnerabilities.
A 403 Forbidden or access denied error is observed when trying to create the symbolic link, indicating insufficient permissions in the temp directory (unlikely on standard setups).
The WER service terminates unexpectedly after the attempted operation, suggesting a crash might have been introduced by the symbolic link or race condition.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary deletion of any file on the system with SYSTEM privileges, which can lead to denial-of-service conditions, removal of critical system files, or destabilization of the operating system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate race conditions involving file operations by privileged services in temporary or user-writable directories, especially when symbolic links can be used to redirect these operations.

meta.title: How Netgear meshed(*) up WiFi for Business

affected_components: Web interface of Netgear WiFi Business Pro routers
tech_stack_fingerprints: Netgear WiFi Business Pro router web interface

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an unauthenticated user can access the web interface of a Netgear WiFi Business Pro router or the Netgear Insight platform.
prerequisites: The target device is a vulnerable Netgear WiFi Business Pro router or uses the Netgear Insight platform.
applicability_signals: Access to the Netgear router's web interface or the Netgear Insight portal.
The presence of a login page for device management.
skip_if: The target network is not using Netgear WiFi Business Pro devices.
The target devices have been patched to address the authentication bypass vulnerability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Netgear WiFi Business Pro routers or devices managed by Netgear Insight on the target network.
Step 2: Access the web interface of the Netgear router or the Netgear Insight portal to confirm the presence of a login page.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access privileged API endpoints directly without authentication. The writeup mentions accessing `/api/v1/device/info` and other related endpoints.
Step 2: Observe the response from these API calls. If unauthenticated access is allowed to these endpoints, it indicates a bypass.
Step 3: If direct API access is blocked, attempt to use the known weak credentials or bypass mechanisms mentioned in the writeup, although the writeup focuses on direct API access.
Step 4: Leverage the information gained from the unauthenticated API calls to further explore the device's configuration or manage it without proper credentials.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify HTTP requests to the target API endpoints.
curl: For direct API requests to test endpoints.
Nmap: For network scanning and host discovery.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK responses from API endpoints that should require authentication.
Retrieval of sensitive device information (e.g., device list, network status, client lists) without valid credentials.
The ability to view or potentially modify device settings through these unauthenticated API calls.
failure_indicators: HTTP 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses for all API calls.
No sensitive information is returned, or generic error messages are displayed.
The web interface requires authentication for all actions.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can bypass authentication and gain access to sensitive device information, potentially control the network, or disrupt its operation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test critical API endpoints for authentication bypass vulnerabilities, especially those related to device information and management, even if a traditional login form is present.

meta.title: Cors Blimey: The power of chaining CORS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user's sensitive information (like email) is accessible via an API endpoint that uses CORS, and the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header is not strictly configured.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session on the target application.
applicability_signals: An API endpoint returns sensitive user data.
The server responds with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header.
The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header reflects a wildcard or allows origins other than the expected ones, or allows `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
skip_if: The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header is strictly whitelisted to only the application's own origin.
The API endpoint does not return sensitive user data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to sensitive user profile pages (e.g., /account) and observe network requests for API calls retrieving personal data.

Step 2: Use Burp Suite's live task feature to scan for CORS issues by sending requests with random subdomain origins (e.g., `https://<random_subdomain>.target.com`). Look for `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` headers in the responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an API endpoint that returns sensitive user data (e.g., `/details/account` or `/details/accountDetails`).
Step 2: Construct a request to this endpoint from a malicious origin (e.g., `https://attacker-controlled.com`) with the `Origin` header set to this malicious domain.
Step 3: If the server responds with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://attacker-controlled.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`, the sensitive data can be exfiltrated.
Step 4: (Optional, for XSS chaining) If the response can be injected into an XSS vulnerability, exploit that to achieve a stronger impact.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /details/account HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Origin: https://attacker-controlled.com

GET /details/accountDetails HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Origin: https://attacker-controlled.com
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for its live task feature for CORS scanning.
Custom script: To automate sending requests from various attacker-controlled origins and parsing responses.
success_indicators: Response contains `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://attacker-controlled.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
The response body contains sensitive information of the victim user (e.g., email address) which can be captured by the attacker's controlled origin.
failure_indicators: Response contains `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null` or a strict whitelist that does not include the attacker's origin.
The API endpoint returns an error or a generic response unrelated to sensitive user data.
No `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` or `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` headers are returned in a way that allows exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can exfiltrate sensitive user data (e.g., email addresses, PII) from any authenticated user by leveraging a misconfigured CORS policy. This can be chained with other vulnerabilities like XSS for further impact.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify CORS configurations, especially the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` headers. Ensure strict whitelisting of trusted origins and avoid wildcard or overly permissive settings.

meta.title: Alternative link

tech_stack_fingerprints: X-Frame-Options header present (implied by discussion of same-origin policy)
Standard HTTP headers (Server, Content-Type, Connection, Content-Length)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application makes requests to a different origin and CORS is configured to allow origins other than the strict whitelist.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session in the target application.
applicability_signals: Observing requests made to different origins for sensitive data (e.g., GET /details/account).
Burp Suite's CORS scanner or manual testing reveals a permissive Access-Control-Allow-Origin header in response to a crafted Origin header.
The server reflects a custom subdomain in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.
skip_if: CORS is strictly configured with an exact origin match and does not reflect custom origins.
The server does not send Access-Control-Allow-Origin or Access-Control-Allow-Credentials headers at all.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target application and identify API endpoints or AJAX requests that fetch sensitive user data.

Step 2: Use Burp Suite's Scanner or manually send requests to these endpoints with a crafted 'Origin' header set to a non-existent or attacker-controlled subdomain (e.g., Origin: https://attacker-controlled.target.com).

Step 3: Analyze the response headers for 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials'. If 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' reflects the attacker-controlled subdomain and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' is 'true', CORS is misconfigured.
Step 4: Investigate other subdomains or functionalities for potential chaining vulnerabilities, such as the /details/email change functionality mentioned in the writeup.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an endpoint that reveals sensitive user information (e.g., GET /details/account) and requires authentication.
Step 2: Craft an HTML page with JavaScript that makes a `fetch` or `XMLHttpRequest` request to the sensitive endpoint.
Step 3: Set the `Origin` header in the JavaScript request to a valid reflected subdomain found during recon (e.g., `https://attacker-controlled.target.com`).
Step 4: Set the `credentials` option to `'include'` in the fetch/XHR request to send cookies.
Step 5: Execute the HTML page in a browser. The JavaScript will attempt to retrieve the sensitive data (e.g., email) from the target domain.

Step 6: If a change email functionality exists (e.g., POST /email/changeEmail), attempt to trigger it using a cross-site request with the stolen session information or by exploiting a CSRF vulnerability on that specific endpoint.
payloads_or_patterns:
Crafted POST request for email change (requires further analysis of parameters):
POST /email/changeEmail HTTP/1.1
Host: subdomain.target.com
Origin: https://attacker-controlled.target.com

old_email=<USER_EMAIL>&new_email=<NEW_EMAIL>&_csrf=<CSRF_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Scanner for CORS, Repeater/Intruder for crafting requests)
Browser Developer Tools (for observing network requests and JavaScript execution)
success_indicators: The JavaScript code successfully retrieves sensitive user data (e.g., email) from the target domain and logs it to the console or sends it to an attacker-controlled server.
A change email request (if chained) is successfully processed, resulting in an email change for the victim's account.
failure_indicators: The browser console shows CORS errors or network requests return 403 Forbidden, indicating the Origin is not allowed or there's a CSRF token mismatch.
The Access-Control-Allow-Origin header is not reflected or does not match the crafted Origin. Suggestion: Investigate other subdomains or look for vulnerabilities that do not rely on wildcard CORS.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate sensitive user data (e.g., email address) from authenticated user accounts. This can be chained with other vulnerabilities like CSRF on sensitive endpoints (e.g., change email) to perform unauthorized actions on behalf of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header for CORS. It should use a strict whitelist of trusted origins and not reflect arbitrary or wildcard origins, especially when 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' is set to 'true'.

meta.title: Account Takeover Scenario 3: Exploiting Signup Without Verification

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows the creation of an account with an already existing email address without any verification mechanism.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to or can predict the victim's email address.
applicability_signals: Signup endpoint accepts an email that has already been registered.
No email verification (e.g., confirmation link) is sent upon account creation.
skip_if: Signup endpoint rejects already registered emails.
Email verification is mandatory before account activation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to register an account with a known email address.
Step 2: Observe if the application flags the email as already in use or proceeds with registration.
Step 3: Verify if an email verification link is sent to the registered email address.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an account using the victim's email address. The server should accept this registration without error.

Step 2: (Optional) If the attacker has prior knowledge of potential sensitive information, create a new account with a different email address and populate it with dummy sensitive data (e.g., credit card details, shipping address).
Step 3: Create a second account using the victim's email address.
Step 4: Log in to the second account created with the victim's email address and check if it reflects the sensitive information entered in Step 2 (if performed).
Step 5: If sensitive information is reflected, an account takeover has occurred, as the attacker has effectively overwritten the victim's account.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a logic flaw, not payload-based)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and replaying requests)
success_indicators: The second account created with the victim's email address displays previously entered sensitive information (e.g., credit card details, shipping address) that belongs to the victim.
The attacker can log in to the victim's account using credentials they set during their account creation.
failure_indicators: The signup process for the victim's email address returns an error indicating the email is already in use. → Pivot: Look for other vulnerabilities that might allow account enumeration or takeover.
No sensitive data is reflected in the attacker's account after registration. → Pivot: Investigate other functionalities for potential information leakage or account manipulation.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains full control over the victim's account, including access to personal information, financial data, and the ability to perform actions on behalf of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Implement email verification for all new account signups to prevent account squatting and subsequent takeovers when an attacker can register with an already-used email address.

meta.title: Stored XSS Leads to Plaintext Password Disclosure

target_profile.technology: Generic Web Application (uses OData API, Basic Authentication)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows uploading arbitrary files as profile pictures and the uploaded content is rendered as HTML without proper sanitization, and authentication cookies are stored in a format accessible via JavaScript.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
The target application must host user profile images on the same domain as the web application.
The authentication mechanism must be HTTP Basic Authentication, with credentials encoded in a cookie accessible to JavaScript.
The application must accept 'text/html' as a valid MIME type for image uploads.
applicability_signals: Profile image upload functionality present.
Intercepted upload request shows Base64 encoded image data and a 'ProfilePictureMIME' parameter.
The application serves uploaded profile images directly as URLs.
The domain serving profile images is the same as the main web application domain.
An authentication cookie (e.g., 'AUTHH') is present and appears to be Base64 encoded.
skip_if: File uploads are strictly validated against known image formats and MIME types, rejecting 'text/html'.
Uploaded images are always served from a different domain or subdomain that cannot execute JavaScript.
Authentication uses secure, HTTP-only cookies or server-side sessions not accessible via JavaScript.
The application employs robust HTML sanitization on rendered content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the web application to identify any file upload functionalities, particularly for user profile customization.
Step 2: Use a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept requests made during file uploads. Examine request parameters, especially those related to file type or content.
Step 3: Identify if the uploaded file content is Base64 encoded and if there's a parameter specifying the MIME type (e.g., 'ProfilePictureMIME').
Step 4: Note the URL where the uploaded profile image is served. Verify if it's on the same domain as the web application and if it's accessible without authentication.
Step 5: While authenticated, examine accessible cookies using browser developer tools or JavaScript. Look for cookies that might contain authentication tokens and check if they are Base64 encoded.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML file containing JavaScript to extract the 'AUTHH' cookie, decode it, split it into username and password, and display them.
Step 2: Base64 encode the crafted HTML file.
Step 3: Intercept a profile picture upload request, replace the original image data with the Base64 encoded malicious HTML, set 'ProfilePictureMIME' to 'text/html', and send the request.
Step 4: Obtain the URL to the uploaded malicious HTML file.

Step 5: Visit the URL of the malicious HTML file while authenticated to the web application. The JavaScript will execute, extract the 'AUTHH' cookie, decode the credentials, and display them on the page.
Step 6: (For actual exploitation) Modify the JavaScript to send the extracted username and password to an attacker-controlled server instead of displaying them.
payloads_or_patterns:
Password Display by bad5ect0r

Your username is:

Your password is:
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, particularly during file uploads.
CyberChef: For Base64 decoding and other encoding/decoding operations.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect cookies and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: The malicious HTML file renders in the browser when accessed via its URL.
The JavaScript successfully extracts, decodes, and displays the username and password from the 'AUTHH' cookie.
failure_indicators: The uploaded file is rejected due to an invalid MIME type. -> Try fuzzing the 'ProfilePictureMIME' parameter with other common HTML/text types.
The uploaded file is displayed as raw text/code instead of rendering HTML. -> This indicates potential HTML sanitization is in place, or the Content-Type header is not being respected for rendering.
The 'AUTHH' cookie is not found or is not Base64 encoded in a way that yields credentials. -> This suggests a different authentication mechanism is in use or the cookie is not exploitable.

The uploaded HTML file does not execute JavaScript. -> Check if the file is served with the correct Content-Type (e.g., 'text/html'), and if there are any Content Security Policy (CSP) headers blocking script execution.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can steal plaintext credentials (username and password) of any user who views their profile picture, leading to account takeover. This is due to Stored XSS allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution within the context of another user's browser session.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always treat user-uploaded content as potentially malicious. Sanitize and validate all user inputs, especially when they are rendered in the browser. Ensure that file upload MIME types are strictly enforced and do not allow rendering of executable content like HTML.

meta.title: $3000 Bug Bounty Award from Mozilla for a successful targeted Credential Hunt

tech_stack_fingerprints: Phabricator instance (inferred from use of arcanist and 'api-' tokens)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target utilizes Phabricator and its API tokens (especially those starting with 'api-' or 'cli-') might be exposed in publicly accessible locations like GitHub repositories.
prerequisites: Discovery of potential Phabricator API tokens in public code repositories or other accessible locations.
applicability_signals: Existence of Phabricator instances (indicated by its use in the organization).
Presence of development tools like 'arcanist' which commonly use API tokens.
Observation of API token formats (e.g., 'api-xxxx', 'cli-xxxx') in discovered code.
skip_if: No Phabricator instances are found to be in use by the target organization.
No API tokens or related development tools are discoverable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target organization's use of Phabricator and associated tools like Arcanist via public information or job postings.
Step 2: Search public code repositories (e.g., GitHub) for potential API tokens used by the target organization, specifically looking for patterns like 'api-' or 'cli-' followed by random characters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a discovered Phabricator API token (e.g., 'api-xxxx').
Step 2: Construct a request to the Phabricator 'user.whoami' API endpoint using the discovered token.
Step 3: Send a POST request to `https://<phabricator_instance>/api/user.whoami` with the `-d api.token=<discovered_token>` parameter.
tools_recommended: GitHub/other code repository search functionality (e.g., GitHub Code Search, `git grep`), cURL for making API requests.
success_indicators: The `user.whoami` API returns a successful response (e.g., HTTP 200 OK) indicating the token is valid and impersonates a user within the target organization.
failure_indicators: The API returns an error (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found) or a generic error message, indicating the token is invalid or the API endpoint is not accessible. This might lead to trying different token formats or searching for alternative API endpoints.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of valid API tokens allows an attacker to impersonate legitimate users of the Phabricator instance, potentially gaining access to sensitive code, bug reports, credentials, and other internal information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Actively hunt for API tokens and other credentials in public code repositories and developer-facing tools, as automated scanners may miss these context-specific exposures.

meta.title: How I made $10K in bug bounties from GitHub secret leaks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When searching for sensitive information within publicly accessible code hosted on GitHub, including repositories and gists.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target domains, subdomains, or patterns associated with a company.
applicability_signals: Availability of GitHub Code Search.
Target companies with active bug bounty programs.
Identification of less common subdomains or domain patterns for a target company.
skip_if: Target company does not have a bug bounty program.
No public repositories or gists are found for the target.
GitHub Code Search is unavailable or heavily restricted.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target domains/subdomains using OSINT tools like Subbrute, ThreatCrowd, or Censys.io.
Step 2: Craft GitHub Code Search dorks using identified domains, patterns (e.g., `*.company.com`), or common secret-related keywords (e.g., `"Authorization: Bearer"`, `api_key`, `password`).
Step 3: Use the `filename:` qualifier to target specific file types known to contain secrets (e.g., `filename:vim_settings.xml`).
Step 4: Explore commit history for repositories using specific dorks (e.g., by cloning the repo and using a script or by using GitHub's commit search.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute crafted GitHub Code Search queries targeting sensitive information (API keys, passwords, customer data).
Step 2: Analyze search results for potential secrets, paying attention to file content and context.
Step 3: For `vim_settings.xml` specifically, clone the repository and run the provided script to analyze its entire copy-paste history.
Step 4: If secrets are found, determine the associated company and report them through their respective bug bounty program.
tools_recommended: GitHub Code Search
GitHound (for automated scanning and exploration)
Subbrute (for subdomain enumeration)
ThreatCrowd (for domain intelligence)
Censys.io (for SSL certificate analysis)
Git clone and custom scripts (for analyzing commit history)
success_indicators: Discovery of API keys, passwords, certificates, PII, or other sensitive credentials.
A valid bug bounty report accepted by a program.
Receiving a bounty payout.
failure_indicators: No relevant results returned by GitHub Code Search (suggests refinement of dorks or target selection needed).
Results contain false positives or non-sensitive information (suggests more specific search queries or context analysis).
No bug bounty program exists for the identified vulnerable company (requires finding another target or reporting via other channels if applicable).

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive credentials such as API keys, passwords, and customer data, potentially leading to unauthorized access, data breaches, financial loss (e.g., AWS charges), and reputational damage for the targeted organizations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Continuously scan public code repositories and gists for leaked secrets using precise search queries and domain reconnaissance, and always check commit histories for past exposures.

meta.title: Pentesting Cisco SD-WAN Part 2: Breaking Routers
meta.category: OS command injection, Security code review

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When SSH access to a vEdge router is available via extracted credentials (e.g., from a compromised vManage) and the target's SSH server on port 830 uses a `ForceCommand` directive that wraps the `scp` command with a script that uses `eval` on `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`.
prerequisites: SSH access to the vEdge router on port 830 with valid credentials.

The SSH server's configuration file (`/tmp/chassis/local/rp/chasfs/rp/0/0/etc/ncsshd/ncsshd_mgmt_persistent.conf`) must contain `ForceCommand /bin/mcp_pkg_wrap rp_base /usr/binos/conf/netconf-subsys.sh`.
The `/bin/mcp_pkg_wrap` script must evaluate commands starting with `scp` directly.
applicability_signals: Successful SSH login on port 830.
Executing `ssh -p 830 admin@<TARGET_IP> "scp||id"` results in the 'usage: scp...' output, indicating command injection is possible.
Presence of the SUID binary `/tmp/etc/bexecute`.
skip_if: SSH connection on port 830 is refused or requires different authentication.
Executing `scp||id` does not reveal the `scp` usage string, indicating the wrapper script does not use `eval` or the `scp` prefix check fails.
The `/bin/mcp_pkg_wrap` script does not perform `eval` on `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` for `scp` commands.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain valid SSH credentials for a vEdge router, likely by compromising a vManage instance.
Step 2: Connect to the target vEdge router via SSH on port 830.
Step 3: Execute `ssh -p 830 admin@<TARGET_IP> "id"` to confirm successful authentication and gather user information.
Step 4: Execute `ssh -p 830 admin@<TARGET_IP> "scp||id"` to check for command injection vulnerability via the `scp` command.
attack_steps: Step 1: If command injection is confirmed via `scp||id`, execute `ssh -p 830 admin@<TARGET_IP> "scp 2>/dev/null|| /bin/bash -i"` to obtain an interactive shell.
Step 2: Once an interactive shell is obtained, execute `find / -xdev -perm -4000 2>/dev/null` to identify SUID binaries.
Step 3: Investigate the SUID binary `/tmp/etc/bexecute`. Execute `/tmp/etc/bexecute -c /usr/binos/conf/install_show.sh` to verify its functionality and potential for privilege escalation.
Step 4: Further analysis of `/tmp/etc/bexecute` and its associated configuration files (`/usr/binos/conf/uicmd.conf`) to identify other exploitable scripts for privilege escalation.
tools_recommended: SSH client
Standard Linux/Unix command-line tools (find, cat, ls)
success_indicators: Receiving the output of the `id` command when executing `scp||id`.
Obtaining an interactive bash shell prompt after executing `scp 2>/dev/null|| /bin/bash -i`.
Successful execution of `/tmp/etc/bexecute` with a specified script path, indicating the SUID binary is functional.
failure_indicators: SSH connection refused or authentication failed: Ensure correct credentials and port are used.

No 'usage: scp...' output after `scp||id`: The wrapper script logic or `ForceCommand` directive is different. Explore other potential command injection vectors or pivot to exploiting vManage directly.
Interactive shell not obtained: The redirection or payload might be blocked by an unintended filter. Try alternative shell spawning commands or examine the wrapper script behavior more closely.

playbooks.impact: Gain an interactive shell on the Cisco SD-WAN vEdge router, potentially leading to full compromise of the device. Identification and exploitation of SUID binaries like `/tmp/etc/bexecute` can lead to root privileges, allowing for configuration changes, data exfiltration, or further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When SSH command wrappers use `eval` on user-supplied input, even seemingly benign commands like `scp` can be leveraged for OS command injection, enabling interactive shell access.

meta.title: DOM-Based XSS at accounts.google.com by Google Voice Extension.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability applies when a user interacts with accounts.google.com and has the Google Voice Extension installed. The exploit relies on how the extension handles data passed through URLs.
prerequisites: Google Voice Extension is installed in the user's browser.
applicability_signals: The target is accounts.google.com
The presence of the Google Voice Extension is suspected or known.
skip_if: The Google Voice Extension is not installed.
The browser does not process URL parameters in the manner described by the vulnerability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the Google Voice Extension is active or has specific functionalities related to URL handling on accounts.google.com.
Step 2: Analyze the JavaScript code of the Google Voice Extension for vulnerable functions that process URL parameters without proper sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that includes a malicious payload in a parameter that the Google Voice Extension is known to process.
Step 2: Trick a victim user into clicking this crafted URL, leading them to accounts.google.com.
Step 3: Observe if the payload is executed in the victim's browser context.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload structure (actual payload would depend on the specific vulnerability in the extension): <TARGET_URL>/?param=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect DOM, JavaScript, and network requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and craft malicious URLs.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript, such as an alert box displaying 'accounts.google.com'.
Any observable client-side script execution that originates from user-controlled input.
failure_indicators: No JavaScript execution occurs, or the payload is simply displayed as plain text.
A 403 Forbidden or other error response, indicating WAF or server-side protection.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the user's session on accounts.google.com. This could lead to session hijacking, credential theft, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze browser extensions for DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities, especially when they interact with sensitive domains like account management pages. Pay close attention to how extensions process URL parameters and other user-controlled input.

meta.title: Stored XSS on biz.waze.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can input text that is later rendered on a page accessible to other users.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated to biz.waze.com.
applicability_signals: Ability to input text into fields on biz.waze.com that is then displayed in a rendered context, potentially to other users.
skip_if: No user-controllable input fields are present or all input is properly sanitized and escaped, preventing XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse biz.waze.com as an authenticated user and identify all input fields.
Step 2: For each input field, analyze if the input is reflected in the response or rendered on a page, especially in a context that could be viewed by others.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject a simple XSS payload like `<script>alert(1)</script>` into an identified input field.
Step 2: Save the input and navigate to where the content is rendered.
Step 3: Observe if the JavaScript executes. If not, try encoding the payload or using different XSS vectors.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying requests, and for identifying potential input points.
success_indicators: The JavaScript alert box pops up when the affected content is viewed.
failure_indicators: The input is displayed as plain text and the script tag is not executed. This indicates proper sanitization or escaping. Pivot to trying different XSS payloads and encodings.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject malicious JavaScript into biz.waze.com, which can then be executed in the browsers of other users who view the affected content. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, unauthorized actions, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controllable input fields for Stored XSS vulnerabilities, especially on administrative or collaboration platforms where content is shared among users.
